title: Declaring & Assigning
description: How to create variables and give them values.

slides.title: Declaring Variables 📝
slides.content:
To define a variable in C++, you must specify its **type** and give it a **name**.

Syntax:
`type variableName = value;`

For example, to store a whole number, we use the `int` type.

slides.title: Example
slides.content: Below, we declare a variable of type `int` (integer) named `score` and assign it the value `10`.

quizData.question: Which of these correctly declares an integer named 'age' and assigns it 25?
quizData.explanation: To declare a variable, you need the type (int), name (age), the assignment operator (=), the value (25), and a semicolon (;).

slides.title: Assigning Later ⏳
slides.content: You don't have to assign a value immediately. You can declare a variable first and assign it a value later on.

slides.title: Re-assigning Variables 🔄
slides.content: If you assign a new value to an existing variable, it will overwrite the previous value.

slides.title: Overwriting Data
slides.codeSnippet:
int myNum = 15;  // myNum is 15
myNum = 10;      // Now myNum is 10
cout << myNum;   // Outputs 10

quizData.question: If you re-assign a newly declared variable some new data, what happens to the old data?

options.text: The old data is overwritten and discarded by the new data.

quizData.explanation: Variables hold one value at a time (for primitive types). Re-assigning completely overwrites the old value.
